title: The Utharvel and the Tightening Clock
recap:
In a single long day in Veranthos, Ming sat still long enough to hear the truth the Shard had been waiting to tell him: that the grief behind his sternum had two textures, and the smaller one belonged not to the Wellspring alone but to every exile who had carried the fragment before him — his teacher's eleven years, his own seventeen, all of it absorbed into something that could no longer be cleanly separated from the man who bore it. A Thornwood shaman named Orrath, burning pine-resin in a courtyard the city's main streets did not acknowledge, gave that condition its name: utharvel — a thing that has been two things long enough to become a third, without yet having a word for what it is. And Orrath went further, speaking aloud what the Unsutured had felt in outline for sixty years from the outside: that the Wellspring itself, after a thousand years bound in the dark, had been dreaming, and was no longer the thing the First Archmages sealed. No one had ever asked what it had become.

Ming left the courtyard with an invitation extended and unanswered, spent the day's remainder mapping what lay between him and the mountains — Conclave checkpoints four days old, a Fault growing louder and wider, something at the Greyveil bridge a veteran teamster refused to describe — and in the tavern's amber light near Lamplighter's Arch, a reassigned Conclave scholar who would not look at him gave him the hardest piece of the day: the fourth ritual component had already reached the staging camp in the foothills. The Archmagister's thirty-one-day window had already begun to close. Ming returned to the Undyed Cloth, oiled his spear-binding in ten careful minutes, and set the weapon against the wall. He had practised his form in a packed-earth yard until the ache was honest and the composure had settled back into place. He had three days. It would have to be enough.
turningPoint: The reassigned Conclave scholar Edric Vaun, staring at the Fault-warmth bleeding through the tavern floor without once meeting Ming's eyes, quietly spoke the words that compressed everything: the fourth ritual component had reached the foothills staging camp two days early. Archmagister Vorn was not waiting thirty-one days — she would move when the geometry allowed it, and the geometry was already assembling itself without him. Three days had just become a number under unknown pressure.

narrativeTurns.playerInput: Sit longer and try to understand whether the Shard's grief is the Wellspring's grief, or something that the Wellspring itself is still carrying
narrativeTurns.narration:
You do not move from the parapet. The city assembles itself around you — the carter, the vendors, the distant bell counting an hour that belongs to everyone else — and you hold very still inside all of it and ask the harder question.

Not what the Wellspring wills. Whether the grief you felt was the Wellspring's grief, or the Shard's grief, or whether that distinction is even real anymore after seventeen years of correspondence. You press two fingers to your sternum again, not opening the door this time — just resting against it. Listening to the structure of what is behind it the way you would listen to a joint in a weapon's haft, checking whether the grain runs true.

What you find is this: the grief has two textures. Beneath the vast oceanic grief — the grief of a will that held one shape for a thousand years — there is a smaller, older grief that is not oceanic at all. It is the grief of something removed. Something that was part of a whole and was taken from the whole and has been carried ever since in a succession of exiled bodies, passed hand to hand across seventeen years, growing slightly more lonely with each transfer because each vessel it inhabited was already lonely and the Shard absorbed that loneliness the way wood absorbs water, slowly, completely, until the grain changes.

The Shard's grief is the Wellspring's grief, but it is also more than the Wellspring's grief. It is the accumulated grief of every exile who carried it. Your teacher's eleven years. Your seventeen. The Shard is not a fragment that has been waiting, neutral and inert, to be returned. It has been changed by what it passed through. Which means — and here the thought lands in your chest like a spear-tip finding purchase — the Wellspring, if it receives the Shard back, receives everything the Shard absorbed. Every exile. Every year of Liang Sorveth writing faster as the sky bled above the Auric Mountains. Every morning you spent alone with a breathing practice and a composure that stood in for the softness you had no one left to give.

The Wellspring asked for a witness. It may not have understood that the witness was already inside what it lost.
narrativeTurns.ask: What do you do with this understanding?
suggestedActions: Return to the Undyed Cloth and write down what you felt — create a record before the clarity fades
Go find Serevaine now and tell her what you learned: the Shard has changed, and the Wellspring will receive more than it sent out
Sit longer still and try to ask the Shard directly whether it wants to return — whether it has a will of its own after seventeen years
Walk to the Sundering Clock in the central plaza and look at what forty-four days remaining actually looks like
Begin moving toward the Conclave district — observe Archmagister Vorn's people from a distance before deciding whether to approach

narrativeTurns.playerInput: Sit longer still and try to ask the Shard directly whether it wants to return — whether it has a will of its own after seventeen years
narrativeTurns.narration:
You stay with the question longer than is comfortable. The city moves around you. A woman crosses the bridge behind you without slowing, her footsteps receding into Tanners' Row. The canal water catches the mid-morning light and breaks it into pieces. You press two fingers to your sternum and you ask the Shard directly, as plainly as you asked Serevaine her arithmetic: do you want to return. After seventeen years do you have a will of your own. And if you do, what is it.

The door does not open.

Not resistance — not a refusal in the way a locked room refuses. More like silence where you expected an answer, the way a student goes quiet not from stubbornness but because the question has arrived before they have the language to answer it. The warmth is present. The Shard is present. But whatever you reached for when you asked — the discrete will, the clean yes or no, the thing that would let you treat this as an honest negotiation between two parties — it does not cohere. The correspondence your teacher wrote about is real, you can feel its texture, but correspondence is not the same as separability. You cannot find where the Shard ends and where seventeen years of Ming begins. You cannot find the seam.

You sit with the failure of the question for a long time. Your fingers are still at your sternum. What you are left with is not an answer but a sharper problem: you cannot ask the Shard what it wills because you cannot yet determine whether the Shard is still a thing that is separate enough from you to will anything independently. The third text said the door and the frame cannot be taken apart without consequence to both. You understood that as a warning about the ritual. You understand it now as something more immediate. You are not carrying a fragment. You are carrying something that may have become, in some way you have no language for, partly yourself. And you do not know if that makes the offering more possible or less.
suggestedActions: Go back to Serevaine and tell her what you found — the Shard cannot answer separately from you, and she needs to know what she is actually asking you to offer
Seek out the Conclave's archive or a scholar who can help you find whatever remains of the other two Archmage texts — read what the first vessels knew
Find the Thornwood shamans in the city — they have lore of voreth and the Wellspring that predates the Covenant, and they may have a different language for what you experienced
Return to Passa and ask about the young Conclave scholar who visited your teacher twice — find out who he was and whether he is still in the city
Walk to the Sundering Clock in the central plaza and stand before it — let the remaining days become real in your body before you decide anything

diceResult.reason: Communicating with a sentient magical artifact about its autonomous will requires navigating complex emotional resonance and arcane connection. The uncertainty lies in whether Ming can pierce through layers of accumulated grief to reach the Shard's authentic desire, and the stakes are high—the answer may reshape his entire purpose.

narrativeTurns.playerInput: Find the Thornwood shamans in the city — they have lore of voreth and the Wellspring that predates the Covenant, and they may have a different language for what you experienced
narrativeTurns.narration:
You open your eyes. The canal water is still moving. You are still here, on the parapet, with a problem that has no clean edges — and a thread you have not yet pulled. Serevaine has Grey-Faction knowledge. The Sealers have Covenant-text. But the Thornwood were in this land before anyone bound anything. They named voreth. They named the Wellspring's grief before the First Archmages decided to solve it. If any lore predates the framing that everyone else is still trapped inside, it belongs to them.

You rise from the parapet, roll your shoulders once to settle the phantom weight of the absent spear, and begin asking.

It takes the better part of the morning. The Thornwood do not announce themselves in Veranthos — they are here because refugees come to gate-cities when the ground shakes, not because they were welcomed. The first person you ask, a hide-seller in the market, looks at you with the flattened expression of a man who has been a foreigner in this city long enough to recognize another one, and says nothing. The second is more useful — a canal-worker with the Thornwood clan-braid half-hidden under a dock-worker's cap who looks at your face for a long moment, then tells you: the Unsutured keep to the south end of Coppervane, in a courtyard behind the rendered-fat chandler, and they do not see people who are not brought.

The third person, a girl of perhaps twelve carrying a basket of tallow-dips through the Stitchway, simply turns and walks ahead of you without being asked, which means she was watching you ask, and you follow her through three back-passages that smell of fat and old smoke into a courtyard that the city's main arteries do not acknowledge.

The courtyard holds a fire — small, efficient, burning something that smells like pine-resin and stone-dust. Around it, four people in the heavy-woven cloaks of the Thornwood hill-country. They look at you the way people look at something they have been warned about in a language that does not have a word for warning. An old man with a face like a map of a hard country sits nearest the fire. The girl with the basket sets it down and fades to the edge of the courtyard without a word. The old man says, in trade-tongue that is accented but entirely precise: 'You smell of the deep fault. And of something older than the fault.' He studies your sternum with the directness of a man who does not need to pretend he is looking at your face. 'Sit down. You have been carrying that for a very long time, and you have questions, and some of them are the right questions. I can tell by the way you are standing that you already know the grief.' He gestures at the ground across the fire from him. 'My name is Orrath. We are called Unsutured because we do not stitch the Wellspring's mouth shut and call that a solution. Sit.'
narrativeTurns.ask: Orrath is waiting. The fire is small and the courtyard is private and he has already named the grief without being told. What do you say first?
suggestedActions: Sit and tell him plainly what you experienced on the bridge — the two textures of grief, the will to be witnessed — and ask if his lore has a name for it
Ask what 'Unsutured' means in practice — whether they want the Wellspring to rupture, or something more specific than that
Ask him what voreth is, in his tradition's terms — not the Sealer definition, the Thornwood one
Show him the name-token and ask if he recognizes Liang Sorveth, or knows who the Far-Eastern scholar was who came here seventeen years ago
Say nothing yet — sit, let the fire settle, and let him lead; he said some of your questions are the right ones, and you want to know which

narrativeTurns.playerInput: Say nothing yet — sit, let the fire settle, and let him lead; he said some of your questions are the right ones, and you want to know which
narrativeTurns.narration:
You sit. Cross-legged, hands open on your knees, the same posture you held on the bridge parapet — receiver's posture, not supplicant's. The fire between you and Orrath is small and speaks in the language of fires that are fed with intention: slow, even, unhurried. The pine-resin smell settles into your clothes. The courtyard is quiet in the specific way of places that have been made quiet deliberately, where the noise of Coppervane exists on the other side of a wall that is not only stone.

Orrath does not rush you. He does not fill the silence with the small talk of a man performing patience. He simply tends the fire with a stick the length of a forearm, making tiny adjustments, and watches the space above your sternum with the directness he has already established. The three others in the courtyard are present but peripheral — they do not watch you the way he watches you. They watch him watching you, which is different.

After a time that has no particular length, Orrath sets the stick across his knees and speaks. His trade-tongue is accented in the way of a man who learned it as a fourth or fifth language, each word chosen with deliberate weight. 'You asked it something this morning,' he says. It is not a question. 'Something you could not get an answer to. Not because it refused. Because the question assumed a separation that is no longer there.' He tilts his head a fraction. 'We have a word for that condition. Not voreth — voreth is what comes before the choosing. This is what comes after the choosing when the boundary of the choosing has dissolved.' He pauses. 'In your language I do not know the word. In ours it is utharvel. A thing that has been two things long enough that it is now a third thing, and the third thing does not yet have a name because no one has looked at it clearly enough to name it.' His eyes come up from your sternum to your face. 'The right questions you already carry are these: not whether the Shard wants to return, and not whether you will survive the offering. Those are the wrong frame.' He holds up one finger. 'The right question is what the third thing — the utharvel, the Ming-Shard — is, and whether the Wellspring has ever received that before. Something that loved it and changed while carrying it.' A second finger. 'And the right question beneath that one is whether the Wellspring, which has also been changing in its binding for a thousand years, is also utharvel now. Also a third thing. Also something that does not yet have a name.' He lowers both fingers and looks at you with the expression of a man who has been waiting a long time to say something to someone who might actually hear it. 'The Covenant texts ask: how do we seal it. The Quietists ask: how do we free it. Both questions assume the Wellspring is still what it was when it was bound.' He pauses once more, and this pause has the weight of something he has been holding for years. 'We do not think it is.'
suggestedActions: Ask Orrath what the Thornwood believe the Wellspring has become — what the utharvel of a bound god looks like after a thousand years
Tell Orrath what you felt on the bridge: the two textures of grief, and the witness-seeking — and ask if the Unsutured have seen this before
Ask what utharvel means for the offering — if the Wellspring is also a third thing, what happens when two unnameable things meet at the fifth-point
Ask Orrath directly: have the Unsutured ever sent someone to the fifth-point before? What happened to them?
Say nothing yet — let him continue; he has more and he is still deciding how much you are ready to hear

narrativeTurns.playerInput: Ask Orrath what the Thornwood believe the Wellspring has become — what the utharvel of a bound god looks like after a thousand years
narrativeTurns.narration:
Orrath is quiet for long enough that the fire speaks between you — a small shift of resin, a breath of pine-smoke moving east. The three others at the courtyard's edge have gone very still. Whatever you just asked, it landed in a different register than the questions that preceded it. You watch Orrath's face make a decision that is not about trust exactly — more like the decision a teacher makes when a student asks the question that means they are finally ready to be told the thing the teacher has been waiting to say for years, and the telling will change the shape of the lesson permanently.

'We do not use the word god,' Orrath says, carefully. 'That word belongs to a frame we do not use. But we have watched the Wellspring for longer than the Covenant has existed, and we have watched it change.' He lifts the stick from his knees and touches it to the fire's edge — not stoking, just contact. 'When it was first bound, a thousand years ago, we felt the binding happen. Our oldest texts say it screamed without sound for seven years. A god-grief. The grief of a will forced into a single form, the way you might force a river into a pipe.' He pauses. 'We expected it to stop grieving. It did not. It grieved for a hundred years, two hundred, and then something happened that we do not have a full account of, only the felt sense of it passed down through every shaman in our line.' He looks up from the fire to your sternum. 'It began to dream.'

Behind your sternum the Shard is entirely still, the stillness of something listening with every part of itself.

'A bound will, given long enough in the binding, does not simply endure. It turns inward. It makes an interior. The Wellspring has spent a thousand years dreaming itself into shapes it was never allowed to be — all the refused forms, all the voreth-colours, all the things it might have become if the First Archmages had not needed it to be one manageable thing.' Orrath sets the stick down across his knees again, with the finality of a man reaching the part of the telling that costs something. 'What we believe the Wellspring has become is this: it is no longer the thing the Archmages bound. It is also no longer the wild unformed thing they bound it from. It has spent a thousand years in the dark becoming something neither of those things. Something that has grief in it, and dreaming in it, and a thousand years of interior life that no one has ever asked about.' His eyes come all the way up to yours. 'You felt the grief this morning. Did you feel what was underneath the grief?'

He waits. It is the first time since you sat down that he has asked you a question rather than naming what he already knows. He is asking because he genuinely does not know the answer — because the Shard was inside you when you listened, and he was not, and whatever is underneath the grief of a thousand-year dreaming god is something the Unsutured have felt only in outline, from the outside, through the Fault-scars and the folded sky, while you were sitting on a bridge with two fingers on your sternum touching it directly.
narrativeTurns.ask: What do you tell Orrath — did you feel something underneath the grief, and if so, what was it?
suggestedActions: Tell him truthfully: beneath the grief you felt will — not yet decided, but present. A will to be witnessed before it is sealed or broken.
Tell him about the two textures of grief — the Wellspring's oceanic grief and the Shard's accumulated exile-grief — and ask if the Unsutured knew the Shard carried something back with it.
Ask him first what he believes is underneath the grief — test whether his account matches yours before you reveal what you felt.
Say nothing yet. Press two fingers to your sternum and open to the Shard here, in the courtyard, with Orrath watching — let him see what you see.

Tell him about utharvel from the inside: that you could not find the seam between yourself and the Shard, and ask whether the Wellspring's dreaming might have done the same thing — become inseparable from the binding it was forced into.

narrativeTurns.playerInput: Say nothing yet. Press two fingers to your sternum and open to the Shard here, in the courtyard, with Orrath watching — let him see what you see.
narrativeTurns.narration:
You press two fingers to your sternum and open the door.

What comes is not what came on the bridge. The bridge was private, the question was patient, the city was indifferent. This is a fire and a watching man and pine-resin smoke and the weight of the Unsutured's attention on your chest — and the Shard, which has been patient all morning, does not open cleanly. The door shifts and something comes through the gap but it comes wrong, the way a word comes wrong in a foreign language when you are tired and the register slips: you feel the grief, the old oceanic grief, and beneath it you reach for what Orrath is asking about, the thing underneath, the interior life, the thousand years of dreaming — and you cannot hold it. It arrives in fragments. Something vast and turned inward. Something that has been making, in the dark, shapes it will never be allowed to show anyone. Something like joy, maybe — but a joy so long unwitnessed it has become indistinguishable from longing, and you cannot tell them apart, and you lose the thread. The warmth behind your sternum flares once, irregular, and subsides. The door closes.

You open your eyes. You do not know when you closed them. Orrath is watching your face with the precise attention of a man who has learned to read the outside of a thing he cannot touch directly, and you know from his expression that what he saw was not a clean transmission. He saw the reach. He saw the failure of the reach. He saw you lose the thread at the exact moment it mattered.

You lower your hand from your sternum. The courtyard is still. The fire is still. You say, in trade-tongue, with the economy of a man reporting an honest result: 'Something like joy. I could not hold it long enough to be certain. It would not come clearly with witnesses.' You pause, and then add the harder part: 'Or I am not yet the right kind of witness.'
narrativeTurns.ask: Orrath absorbs this without visible disappointment — more like a man whose hypothesis has been tested and returned a partial result, which is still a result. The fire breathes between you. What do you do?
suggestedActions: Ask Orrath whether the Unsutured have ever found a way to hear the Wellspring's interior more clearly — a practice, a place, a condition under which it speaks
Tell Orrath the full truth of what you felt on the bridge this morning: the grief, the will to be witnessed, and the possibility that the Shard has absorbed every exile who carried it
Ask Orrath directly: he said he had been waiting for someone with the right questions — how long, and what told him you were coming
Sit with the failure quietly and let Orrath speak next without prompting
Ask whether the Unsutured would accompany you to the Wellspring itself — whether proximity to the Fault-scars would change what you can hear

diceResult.reason: Opening yourself to the Shard is a meaningful magical act with uncertain outcome—maintaining control while exposing your inner perception to both the Shard and Orrath's scrutiny, especially after the morning's contact. What emerges (clarity, vulnerability, chaos, revelation) hangs on magical finesse.

narrativeTurns.playerInput: We sit and have food in silent. Then I ask if they want to go to the Willspring with me.
narrativeTurns.narration: Passa's food arrives without explanation — the girl with the tallow-basket reappears from somewhere with a cloth bundle that turns out to contain flatbread, hard cheese, and two small clay cups of something warm and bitter that the Thornwood drink the way you drink dark morning tea. You eat in silence. The fire speaks. Orrath eats with the same economical attention he gives everything else, and the three others eat without looking at you, which is its own form of acceptance. The food is plain and adequate and you finish all of it, because the body that carries the Shard is still a body. When the cups are empty and the cloth bundle is folded and the fire has settled into its quieter afternoon register, you set your hands on your knees and look at Orrath across the coals and say, in trade-tongue, with the plainness of a man stating a route: 'I am going to the Wellspring. Not to the fifth-point of the ritual geometry. Not yet. To listen — to be the kind of witness it asked for, from closer than a bridge in Coppervane.' A pause. 'I am asking if you want to come.' The courtyard holds the question. Orrath does not answer immediately. He looks at your sternum, then at your face, then at the fire, with the unhurried attention of a man checking three different instruments before he reads the weather. One of the three others at the edge of the courtyard — a younger woman with the hill-country braid and a stillness that suggests she has been listening to every word since you arrived — looks up from the coals and looks at Orrath with an expression that is not quite a question and not quite a plea. Orrath sets his cup down. He says, slowly: 'The Unsutured have not walked to the Auric Mountains in eleven years. The last time, the Sealers turned us back at the foothills.' Another pause. 'But you are carrying the inside of it. And I have been watching the outside for sixty years and I have never spoken to anyone who touched what you touched this morning.' He is quiet for a long moment. 'This is not a small thing you are asking.'
narrativeTurns.ask: Orrath has not said no. He has said it is not a small thing, and he is still weighing it across the fire. The younger Thornwood woman is watching him. What do you do?
suggestedActions: Say nothing more — let Orrath finish weighing it; you have asked once, which is enough
Tell him what you felt on the bridge — the grief, and the thing beneath the grief that might have been joy — as fully as you can, so he knows what he is being asked to witness
Acknowledge the risk directly: the Sealers, the forty-four days, the possibility that going to the mountains changes everything before you have decided anything
Look at the younger woman and ask her name — she has been listening since you arrived and her opinion matters to Orrath
Pull out Liang Sorveth's third text and set it on the ground between you — let him see it exists, without opening it

narrativeTurns.playerInput: I am going there in three days. You know how to find me in this city. Then I left and start to dig out the mages intention and the story from their side.
narrativeTurns.narration:
You stand, which closes the question without closing the door. 'Three days,' you say. 'You know where to find me.' Orrath looks at you from across the dying fire with the expression of a man who has been given a thing he was not certain he would ever receive, and is now uncertain what it weighs. He does not say yes. He does not say no. He says: 'Three days.' And the way he says it — the weight he gives the number — tells you it is neither acceptance nor refusal but something more honest than either: a shaman checking whether the ground will hold before he steps onto it. You leave the courtyard the way you entered it, through the back-passages, and the girl with the tallow-basket is not there to guide you out, which means you find it yourself, which means you were watching on the way in. You were.

The Conclave district is north of Coppervane, past Lamplighter's Arch — the part of Veranthos where the ley-lanterns run brightest and the blue threadwork is not embroidered on cuffs but hung above doorways like flags. You go there with the economy of a man who has decided on a route, and you find what you expected to find: the Arcane College is a working institution under siege conditions, which means it is both fortified and porous. Sealed doors and nervous functionaries and couriers moving in and out with the compressed urgency of people who know the Clock is running. A partial success at reading the shape of it — enough to see the outline, not enough to see the interior. What you read clearly: the Sealers are afraid, and their fear has curdled into motion. They are not deliberating any longer. They are executing. You overhear a fragment of argument near the eastern gate between two robed figures — one senior, one junior — about the fourth shrine-component, whether it has been secured, whether the timeline allows for the journey. The senior figure says: 'The Archmagister has already decided. The ritual window opens in thirty-one days.' What you do not read clearly: who else the Conclave has spoken to, what they know about the fifth component, whether Vorn has any doubt at all about her incomplete text. The junior robed figure glances at you once — a foreigner loitering near a gate — and you move on before the glance becomes a question.
narrativeTurns.ask: You are standing in the Conclave district with a partial picture: thirty-one days to the ritual window, the Archmagister already committed, a fourth shrine-component being secured. You have three days before Orrath answers. What do you do?
suggestedActions: Find a way inside the Conclave — as a tradesperson, petitioner, or curiosity — and get closer to Vorn or her inner circle
Return to Passa's and study Liang Sorveth's third text for anything about the Sealers' ritual that might give you leverage
Send word to Serevaine — she operates between factions and may already know what the Conclave's full plan is
Find the junior robed figure who glanced at you and follow them — a nervous junior mage may be easier to read than the institution
Go to the Sundering Clock in the central plaza and watch who gathers there — factions reveal themselves around symbols

diceResult.reason: Gathering information from mages requires reading people, asking the right questions, and interpreting responses—uncertain outcome against resistant or guarded sources.

narrativeTurns.playerInput: I decided to get my weapon first as my journey will begin in three days.
narrativeTurns.narration:
You turn away from the Conclave district and walk back through Lamplighter's Arch toward the Undyed Cloth with the particular purpose of a man who has finished one kind of work and started another. The spear is in your room, wrapped in oiled cloth and leaned against the wall behind the door — you left it there this morning because a foreigner carrying a six-foot weapon draws a different kind of attention than a foreigner asking questions. You take the stairs two at a time, which is not composure exactly but is its cousin.

The room is as you left it. The spear is where you left it. You unwrap the oiled cloth and take the haft in both hands and stand with it for a moment in the narrow space between bed and window, not in form, just holding it — the weight redistributing itself through your palms the way it always does, the grain of the ash familiar enough that your hands know it in the dark. The Crane-in-Storm spear form was built around a body that was already carrying something it did not know it was carrying, which means the form was shaped, at least in part, by the Shard's presence in Liang Sorveth before you, and you do not know yet what that means for the form you learned from him. You set the thought aside. The weapon is sound. The tip needs no work. The binding at the lower third is slightly dry and you take ten minutes with the oil and a strip of cloth to address it properly, because three days is enough time to do this correctly and a spear that fails at altitude in the cold is a different problem than a spear that needs attention now.

When it is done you stand the spear against the wall and look at it. It is the one thing you brought across the sea that belongs entirely to you — not a text someone wrote to instruct your choices, not a token pressed into your hands by a woman keeping faith with a dead man's instructions, not a fragment of something ancient lodged behind your sternum without your consent. Yours. The composure settles back into place around that fact and stays there.

Three days. The Conclave's ritual window opens in thirty-one. Vorn has committed to an incomplete text and does not know it. Serevaine wants the Shard delivered. Orrath has not said no. The Wellspring wants a witness. And you have a weapon, a third text, a name-token, and seventeen years of a Crane-in-Storm form built around a door. You have been in worse positions, though you are having difficulty at this exact moment remembering when.
narrativeTurns.ask: The afternoon is yours — three days before you leave for the Auric Mountains, and several threads still unresolved in the city. What do you do?
suggestedActions: Go to Serevaine — you have questions she has not yet answered, and she has been waiting longer than Orrath.
Return to the Conclave district with a different approach — find the young Sealer who visited Liang Sorveth seventeen years ago.
Read the third text again, now that you have new language for it — utharvel, the Wellspring's dreaming, what Orrath told you.
Drill the Crane-in-Storm form in the courtyard of the Undyed Cloth — let the body process what the mind has been carrying all day.
Go to the Fault-scar near the Auroch Gate and open to the Shard in a place where the boundary is thin — try again for the thing underneath the grief.

narrativeTurns.playerInput: Practice in the yard, get myself ready.
narrativeTurns.narration:
The Undyed Cloth has a yard — barely. A strip of packed earth behind the kitchen, bounded by a stone wall on two sides and the back of a dyer's shed on the third, smelling of old mordant and clean dirt. Passa is somewhere inside. The yard is empty. It is enough.

You shed your outer coat, take the spear in both hands, and begin.

Not combat forms. Not the high-intensity sequences that end with the tip at a throat. The root work first — the Crane-in-Storm foundation that your teacher built the entire form around, which means the foundation your teacher built around a door he was already planning to pass to you, which you know now and which you hold at a distance while your feet find their positions in the packed earth. The body does not need to settle the question of Liang Sorveth to do this work. The body knows the form. The body has known it for seventeen years and the fact that it was built around a secret does not unmake what you made of it in the years after. You have thought of this before, with other things. A technique taught by a flawed teacher is still a technique. You keep what works.

You run the first sequence slow — thinking-speed, each position held three full breaths, feeling where the weight sits, where the Shard sits, whether they are in correspondence or in conflict today. They are not in conflict. The warmth behind your sternum tracks the spear-tip the way it sometimes does in long forms, a faint attentiveness, and you let it track without comment. The door stays closed. The Shard does not need to speak into this. It watches, and you work, and the yard is very quiet except for the sound of ash-wood moving through morning air and your breathing, which is four counts, hold, eight counts out, the rhythm so old it requires nothing of you now except that you do not stop.

The second sequence comes faster. The third faster still. By the fourth you are no longer thinking about Orrath or the Conclave's thirty-one-day window or what utharvel means for the geometry of the offering — you are in the form and the form is in you and the spear is a six-foot extension of a question your body has been asking since you were nineteen years old and exiled and standing on a dock with everything taken and the form still in your hands, still intact, still yours. You practice until your shoulders ache with the good ache of honest work, until the sweat on your back cools in the early afternoon air, until the yard is longer than it was when you started because you have stopped seeing the walls.

You bring the spear back to guard and hold it there. Your breathing is even. Your composure is not the same thing as calm — it never has been — but right now it is close enough that the distinction does not matter. You are ready. Not for the offering, not for the Wellspring's grief or Vorn's incomplete text or Serevaine's arithmetic. Ready for the next three days. That is the right unit of readiness.
narrativeTurns.ask: What do you do with the rest of the afternoon?
suggestedActions: Go to Serevaine — you have not spoken to her since learning the full weight of what she wants, and that conversation is overdue
Read Liang Sorveth's third text again, now that you have new language for what it describes
Return to the canal bridge and try once more to touch the Wellspring's interior — you are warmer now, more settled
Seek out what the Coppervane merchant district knows about the road to the Auric Mountains and what lies between here and the foothills
Rest — you have been carrying a great deal since dawn and the body that holds the Shard is still a body

narrativeTurns.playerInput: Seek out what the Coppervane merchant district knows about the road to the Auric Mountains and what lies between here and the foothills
narrativeTurns.narration:
You wrap the spear in its oiled cloth and lean it against the wall, pull your outer coat back on, and walk north through Tanners' Row into Coppervane with the particular economy of a man conducting reconnaissance. The merchant district in the early afternoon is at its most useful — the morning rush has settled, the serious traders are in back rooms doing serious arithmetic, and the people left visible on the street are the ones who have time to talk, which usually means they have something to say.

You start with cartographers. There is one on the Coppervane main thoroughfare — a narrow shop wedged between a spice importer and a moneychanger, run by a compact woman with ink-stained fingers who looks at you the way cartographers look at everyone: as a potential customer with a specific problem. You buy a route-map of the western roads for two silver marks, which is twice the going rate and which she accepts without comment, and while she rolls it you ask, in trade-tongue, about the road to the Auric Mountains. She tells you what the map already shows — the King's Road west out of the Auroch Gate, three days to the foothills at a good pace, four if the road is soft from Fault-heat, which it has been. She adds, without prompting, that the waystation at Helvrath's Cross is not operating — closed six weeks ago when the warden abandoned it. 'People have been abandoning the road west,' she says, without particular drama. 'Not stupidity. Pragmatism.' She hands you the rolled map.

The second useful conversation happens at a teamster's yard near the Coppervane canal landing, where a broad man named Dusset who runs supply-wagons to the mining concerns in the foothills is willing to talk because you bought him a cup of something warm from the stall across the lane. He has stopped running wagons west for three weeks. The reasons, in order: the road past Helvrath's Cross has Fault-scars that are no longer small and warm but wide and bright and loud, audible at night as a low harmonic that gets into the teeth and makes the horses refuse; there are Conclave checkpoints at two points on the King's Road — established four days ago, with blue-threadwork wardens who turn back commercial traffic and ask questions about cargo; and there is something wrong with the Greyveil bridge, the only reliable crossing of the Sullen River before the foothills, though he cannot say what exactly except that the last driver who came back from it would not describe what he saw and drank for two days after.

You leave Dusset with the map tucked inside your coat and a cleaner picture of what three days of preparation actually needs to address. The road is passable but watched. The Fault is louder and wider than it was. There is something at the Greyveil bridge that a teamster who crosses rivers for a living refused to describe. Checkpoints four days ago means the Sealers have begun securing the approach faster than their thirty-one-day window suggests. You have a spear, an incomplete text, a name-token, and seventeen years of a form built around a door. The next question is whether you solve the checkpoints before you leave, or solve them when you reach them.
narrativeTurns.ask: You have the route and its problems. What do you do with the next three days?
suggestedActions: Go to Serevaine — the checkpoints are four days old and she may have known; find out what else she has not told you
Return to the Undyed Cloth and study Liang Sorveth's third text for anything about the fifth-point geometry or what the offering requires
Find legitimate cover for the journey — a trader, a pilgrim group, anything that gets you through a Conclave checkpoint without questions about cargo
Go to the Conclave district and probe the checkpoint order directly — find out who authorized it and whether Vorn knows about the fifth component yet
Wait and see whether Orrath sends word before three days are up — his answer may change what the journey requires

narrativeTurns.playerInput: Find legitimate cover for the journey — a trader, a pilgrim group, anything that gets you through a Conclave checkpoint without questions about cargo
narrativeTurns.narration: You spend the remainder of the afternoon working the problem. A legitimate cover — a trader's manifest, a pilgrim group, a carter's invoice — is the clean solution, the one that costs nothing at the checkpoint and leaves no memory in a warden's mind. You are methodical about it. You ask at three trading houses about westward caravans; two have suspended western runs entirely, and the third looks at you with the flat eyes of a man who knows a foreigner asking about western cargo is not asking about western cargo. You find a Quietist pilgrim circle that walks to the mountain shrines on a seasonal circuit — but their departure is ten days out and their leader, a small careful woman who asks too many questions about why you are in Veranthos, does not give you the feeling of someone who will be incurious at a checkpoint. You find a herbalist's supply run that goes as far as Helvrath's Cross — but it stopped running when the waystation closed and the driver is not resuming until the road is confirmed passable, which is to say never. By the time the afternoon light has gone copper and the ley-lanterns are warming in their housings along the Coppervane thoroughfare, you have found nothing that holds. Every cover requires either a wait you cannot afford or a dependency on someone who will notice the spear, notice the way you carry yourself, notice the thing behind your sternum that makes the Fault-scar warmth flicker when you stand too long in one place. The city is contracting. The people who move westward freely are the Conclave's own people, and they do not need cover because they are the checkpoint. You stand outside the cartographer's shop with the rolled map inside your coat and the honest result of the afternoon in your hands: you found nothing. The checkpoints will have to be solved when you reach them, not before.
narrativeTurns.ask: The legitimate cover approach failed — the city's westward traffic has dried up and what remains won't serve. You have three days, two Conclave checkpoints on the King's Road, a teamster's warning about the Greyveil bridge, and no clean way through. What do you do?
suggestedActions: Return to Serevaine — she navigates between factions; she may know a route or credentials that bypass the checkpoints entirely
Study the route-map for an off-road alternative — the Fault-scars may have opened new terrain, and a spear master does not need a road
Go back to Dusset the teamster and press him harder on what exactly the driver saw at the Greyveil bridge — the bridge problem may matter more than the checkpoints
Wait at the Undyed Cloth and read the third text again — the passage about the fifth-point geometry may contain something about the approach route
Go to the Conclave district and attempt to use Serevaine's name-token as a credential — risk it now rather than at a checkpoint in the open road

diceResult.reason: Finding and convincing a willing group to provide cover requires persuasion, social navigation, and reading intent—uncertain outcome with meaningful consequences for safe passage.

narrativeTurns.playerInput: I will punch my way out, checkpoint won't be a problem. Get some Intel and the missing piece from Conclave and mages before departure.
narrativeTurns.narration:
You stand outside the cartographer's shop and let the afternoon settle around the failed search. The cover problem has a clean solution: there is no cover to find. The checkpoints are Conclave wardens. Conclave wardens carry orders, not wisdom. A foreigner with a spear and a straight back who does not flinch at their questions is less suspicious than a foreigner with a forged manifest who flinches at their questions. You will walk through them on your own terms or go around them, and you will decide which when you see what is in front of you. The problem is filed and closed.

The missing piece is the harder problem. Not the checkpoints — the Conclave's knowledge. You know they are executing against an incomplete text. You know they have thirty-one days and four shrine-components and no fifth. What you do not know is what else they know: whether Vorn has any surviving doubt, whether there are Sealer scholars who have found the same gap in the Covenant-text that Serevaine found, whether anyone inside the blue threadwork has handled the arithmetic of the fifth component and quietly set it aside because it was inconvenient. This is the intelligence worth carrying west.

You spend the last of the afternoon working the district's edges — not the Conclave district itself, which is locked down and watching, but its penumbra: the ink-merchants who supply the college, the boarding houses where junior scholars stay when the college's own rooms are full, the tavern near Lamplighter's Arch where the Conclave's working staff drink after the late shift. You are economical and patient. You buy two cups of wine you do not drink and listen to conversations you were not invited into. What you gather: the Archmagister has sealed internal debate — two senior scholars who raised questions about the Covenant-text's completeness were reassigned to component-transport duty six days ago, which is the Conclave's version of silencing. One of them, a narrow-faced man called Edric Vaun who now apparently drinks alone at the Lamplighter's Arch tavern after his reassignment, says nothing useful when you sit near him — but he looks at the Fault-scar warmth bleeding through the tavern floor with the expression of a man who has done arithmetic that arrived at a wrong answer and knows it and has been told not to say so. He looks at the warmth for a long time. He does not look at you. But when you stand to leave he says, quietly, in Avelornian you mostly follow: 'If someone were going west, they would want to know the fourth component reached the staging camp two days ago. Thirty-one days was already tight. It is tighter now.' He does not look up from his cup.

You walk back to the Undyed Cloth with this: the Conclave's timeline has compressed. The fourth component is already at the staging camp in the foothills — which means Vorn will not wait the full thirty-one days. She will move when the geometry allows it. You do not know when that is. You know it is sooner than you planned for, and three days is now a number that has to hold against an unknown pressure. You are going west in three days. You will solve the checkpoints when you reach them. You have a spear, a map, a third text, a Thornwood shaman who has not said no, and seventeen years of a form built around a door that is also, it turns out, partly you. It is enough. It has to be enough.
narrativeTurns.ask: Three days remain before you depart. How do you use them — and is there anyone in Veranthos you need to speak to before you go?
suggestedActions: Seek out Serevaine and tell her what you learned — the timeline has compressed; does she have intelligence about the staging camp or the ritual window?
Return to the Unsutured courtyard before departure and give Orrath what you learned from Edric Vaun — he deserves the updated picture before he decides
Spend the three days in preparation only — forms, rest, study the third text again for anything about the staging camp or the fifth-point geometry
Find Edric Vaun again tomorrow and press harder — a man reassigned for asking the right question may be willing to say more with less wine in front of him
Go to the Auroch Gate at dusk and stand where the sky folds — let the Shard orient to the direction of the Wellspring before you commit to the road

imagePrompt: High fantasy illustration, epic and urgent in tone. A lean Far-Eastern man — weathered, dark-eyed, in a foreign-cut travelling coat, bearing the quiet economy of a martial discipline — stands alone in a narrow packed-earth courtyard behind a Veranthos chandler's yard at dusk. He holds a six-foot ash-wood spear at guard, tip raised, the blade catching the last copper light filtering over the stone wall. His right hand shows the spear-callus at the palm. Before him on the ground a small intentional fire burns pine-resin and stone-dust, its smoke rising straight in the still air, its coals dimming toward evening. Across the dying fire, empty: the place where Orrath sat. On the far wall, faint and involuntary, the ghost-warmth of a Fault-scar bleeds through the stone like breath through cloth. The man's posture is composed, not calm — the posture of someone who has received every piece of news the day had to offer and is still standing, oriented west, the road already decided. The illustration style evokes detailed high fantasy book art: rich warm shadow, precise linework, the weight of old stone and firelight against encroaching dark.
